mildew.desc: MILIONCAST2 predicts the sporulation rate by integrating temperature and relative humidity during the dark period. It is an experimental model fitted in controlled environments with British isolates. DOWNCAST is the earlier canonical rule-based (yes/no) model, which the MILIONCAST publication itself showed predicts sporulation worse than random because of its overly strict RH threshold (95%).
vars: Drives the c(T) and m(T) parameters of the Gompertz function.
Threshold ≥92% defines effective sporulation hours; modulates rate via sr=(1−R)^(100−RH).
Astronomical calculation of the dark window (civil dusk to 09:00).
logica: c(T) = maximum log₁₀ sporangia, gamma distribution peaking at 12–14 °C.
m(T) = time to inflection point, inverse gamma with minimum (~5 h) at 10–12 °C.
log₁₀(s+1) = c(T)·exp(−exp(−0.90·(t−m(T)))), with t = hours of RH≥92% in darkness.
Humidity factor sr = (1−0.40)^(100−RH): ≈1 at 100% RH, drops to ~0.03 at 93%.
Classification: strong >10⁴, moderate 10²–10⁴, low 1–10², none <1 sporangia/3 leaves.
mildew.downcast: DOWNCAST (Table 1 of Gilles et al. 2004): (1) previous daytime mean T ≤24 °C, 08–20 h; (2) night T 4–24 °C, 20–08 h; (3) no rain, 01–06 h; (4) RH≥95% continuous, 02–06 h. All must be met.
mildew.supuestos: British parameters not recalibrated to Chile. Hourly integration (the paper uses 10 min). T×RH interaction treated as multiplicative in log scale. Precipitation de-accumulated from the raw record.
refs: Gilles, T., Phelps, K., Clarkson, J.P. & Kennedy, R. (2004). Development of MILIONCAST, an Improved Model for Predicting Downy Mildew Sporulation on Onions. Plant Disease 88(7): 695–702.
Jesperson, G.D. & Sutton, J.C. (1987). Evaluation of a forecaster for downy mildew of onion (Allium cepa L.). Crop Protection 6: 95–103.
de Visser, C.L.M. (1998). Development of a downy mildew advisory model based on downcast. European Journal of Plant Pathology 104: 933–943.

porri.desc: Infection model based on the leaf wetness duration required at each temperature, per the controlled-environment studies of Suheri & Price (2000). A warm-climate pathogen: infection requires moderate-to-warm temperatures, so low pressure is expected in the cold winter of central Chile.
vars: Defines the required wetness threshold and the thermal favorability factor.
logica: Temperature-dependent wetness threshold: ≥8 h at 10–25 °C; ≥16 h at 15 °C; rises sharply below 10 °C; no infection below 6 °C.
Thermal factor: optimum 25–27 °C (=1.0), declining on both sides, ~0 below 10 °C.
Severity (0–4) = min(4, (wetness hours / threshold)·4·thermal factor).
porri.supuestos: Leaf wetness estimated via RH≥90% proxy, not measured. Thresholds derived from studies with non-Chilean isolates.
refs: Suheri, H. & Price, T.V. (2000). Infection of onion leaves by Alternaria porri and Stemphylium vesicarium and disease development in controlled environments. Plant Pathology 49: 375–382.
Everts, K.L. & Lacy, M.L. (1990). The influence of dew duration, relative humidity, and leaf senescence on conidial formation and infection of onion by Alternaria porri. Phytopathology 80: 1203–1207.
Pacific Northwest Pest Management Handbooks — Onion (Allium cepa): Purple Blotch (pnwhandbooks.org).

dauci.desc: Pitblado's TOM-CAST model (1992), the standard for carrot leaf blight. It combines leaf wetness hours and mean temperature during wetness into a daily severity value (DSV, 0–4) that accumulates to an action threshold. Warm-climate pathogen (optimum 28 °C).
logica: Pitblado DSV table: crosses mean wetness T (ranges 13–17, 18–20, 21–25, 26–29 °C) with wetness hours.
Each day contributes a DSV of 0 to 4 per that table.
DSVs accumulate; typical action threshold: 15–20 DSV between sprays.
dauci.supuestos: Wetness via RH≥90% proxy. The original Pitblado table uses measured leaf wetness. Action threshold is referential.
refs: Pitblado, R.E. (1992). The development and implementation of TOM-CAST: A weather-timed fungicide spray program for field tomatoes. Ministry of Agriculture and Food, Ontario, Canada.
Cornell Vegetables — Carrot Leaf Blight Diseases and their Management (vegetables.cornell.edu).
UC IPM — Alternaria Leaf Blight / Carrot: Pest Management Guidelines (ipm.ucanr.edu).

bras.desc: Infection model for the two brassica Alternaria species. Unlike A. porri and A. dauci, A. brassicae infects in cool conditions (as little as 4 h wetness at ~18 °C), making it the only Alternaria with real potential pressure in the Chilean winter. Relevant for cabbage seed production.
logica: Minimum wetness: 4 h. Thermal range 5–30 °C, optimum 15–24 °C.
Thermal factor: 1.0 at 15–24 °C; 0.6 at 10–28 °C; 0.3 outside.
Base severity by wetness hours (4–8 h=1, 8–12=2, 12–18=3, >18=4), ×thermal factor.
bras.supuestos: Wetness via RH≥90% proxy. Sporulation requires RH≥91.5% (A. brassicae) / 87% (A. brassicicola). Extension-literature thresholds, not a fitted equation.
refs: Pacific Northwest Pest Management Handbooks — Cabbage and Cauliflower (Brassica oleracea): Alternaria Diseases (pnwhandbooks.org).
Cornell Vegetables — Alternaria Leaf Spot of Brassicas (vegetables.cornell.edu).
UW Vegetable Pathology — Brassica Alternaria Leaf Spot (vegpath.plantpath.wisc.edu).

squamosa.desc: Onion leaf blight (blast). BOTCAST (Sutton et al. 1986) is a cumulative severity-index system; Carisse et al. (2012) quantified infection efficiency with a Weibull equation of temperature × wetness. Cool-climate pathogen (optimum ~20 °C, near-nil at 30 °C), so it may pose real pressure in autumn-winter.
vars: Leaf wetness proxy. Carisse validated "wetness + RH>90% hours over 72 h" as the best criterion.
logica: Thermal factor: optimum 18–22 °C (=1.0), 10–25 °C (0.7), extremes (0.15–0.4), nil outside 6–29 °C.
Wetness factor: rises from 12 h to 72 h (0.3 → 1.0).
Severity (0–4) = thermal factor · wetness factor · 4.
squamosa.supuestos: Wetness via RH≥90% proxy. Approximation of Carisse's PMI (proportion of maximum infection) to a 0–4 scale. Full BOTCAST also requires airborne inoculum incidence.
refs: Sutton, J.C., James, T.D.W. & Rowell, P.M. (1986). BOTCAST: a forecasting system to time the initial fungicide spray for managing Botrytis leaf blight of onions. Agriculture, Ecosystems & Environment 18(2): 123–143.
Carisse, O., et al. (2012). A new risk indicator for botrytis leaf blight of onion caused by Botrytis squamosa based on infection efficiency of airborne inoculum. Plant Pathology 61: 1154–1164.
Tanner, M.R. & Sutton, J.C. (1981). Effect of leaf wetness duration and temperature on infection of onion leaves by Botrytis squamosa. Phytopathology 71: 565.

cinerea.desc: Polyphagous gray mold that attacks senescing flowers and umbels — of particular relevance for seed production. Unlike B. squamosa, it lacks a consensus BOTCAST-type quantitative model, so a generic infection model based on literature temperature and wetness thresholds is used. Wide thermal range.
vars: Leaf wetness / free-moisture proxy on floral tissue.
logica: Minimum wetness: 4 h. Thermal range 1–30 °C, optimum 15–22 °C.
Thermal factor: 1.0 at 15–22 °C; 0.7 at 8–28 °C; 0.35 outside.
Severity (0–4) = thermal factor · wetness factor · 4.
cinerea.supuestos: Wetness via RH≥90% proxy. Generic model, not B. cinerea-specific. Greater uncertainty than B. squamosa. Strongly influenced by floral-tissue senescence (not captured by the model).
refs: Pacific Northwest Pest Management Handbooks — Botrytis (Gray Mold) host-disease pages (pnwhandbooks.org).
Williamson, B., Tudzynski, B., Tudzynski, P. & van Kan, J.A.L. (2007). Botrytis cinerea: the cause of grey mould disease. Molecular Plant Pathology 8(5): 561–580.
Nota: modelo de infección genérico basado en umbrales T × mojado de literatura; B. cinerea no cuenta con un modelo cuantitativo específico consensuado como BOTCAST.

sclero.titulo: Clarkson et al. 2007 — Carpogenic germination of Sclerotinia
sclero.tipo: Two-phase model (conditioning + germination) with the original coefficients from Clarkson 2007.
sclero.desc: Apothecium production by carpogenic germination of sclerotia. A TWO-PHASE sequential model: first cold conditioning (fast at 5–10 °C), then germination (optimum 18–20 °C). Apothecia release the ascospores that start the epidemic. Replaces the previous 'inoculum always ready' assumption with the real germination process.
vars: Drives both phases: conditioning (cold) and germination (mild). Ideally soil; air proxy here.
Moist-soil proxy (RH≥75%); without moisture the process stalls. Infection needs RH≥80%.
logica: Phase 1 — Conditioning: rc = 0.03273 + 1000·e^(−1.498·T) per day (eq. 2). Accumulates to 1. Nil ≥20 °C.
Phase 2 — Germination: rg = exp(31.12 − 10138/(T+273)) per day (eq. 3). Only after conditioning is complete. Nil ≥25 °C.
After winter, conditioning is assumed complete (Clarkson): the index reflects germination progress.
Index (0–100) = germination(0–1) · daily infection condition (7–25 °C and RH≥80%) · 100.
Germination(0–100) = fraction of apothecia ready.
sclero.supuestos: The model offers THREE comparable temperature variants (like downcast/milioncast): SURFACE (TS00, where sclerotia germinate, noisier), −10 cm (TS10, more stable, preferred variant) and AIR (fallback/comparison). Carpogenic germination occurs in soil, so the soil variants are physiologically more correct than air. IMPROVEMENT (v1.6): now uses soil temperature (TS10, -10 cm from Agromet) for conditioning and carpogenic germination —soil processes—, falling back to air only if the station lacks soil data. Improves fidelity over the previous (air-only) proxy. Implements the ORIGINAL coefficients from Clarkson et al. (2007), isolate 13 (Table 4, eq. 2-3). Validated against the paper's totals (212 days at 5 °C; 72 days at 18 °C). Calibrated on field lettuce (UK) — the closest horticultural model to seed brassicas, but extrapolation to B. oleracea is approximate (no dedicated model exists). Uses air T as a proxy for soil T (vilab has no soil T). The real soil-moisture threshold depends on water potential, simplified here with RH. Local calibration to Chile still pending.
refs: Clarkson, J.P., Phelps, K., Whipps, J.M., Young, C.S., Smith, J.A. & Watling, M. (2007). Forecasting Sclerotinia Disease on Lettuce: A Predictive Model for Carpogenic Germination of Sclerotinia sclerotiorum Sclerotia. Phytopathology 97(5): 621–631.
Clarkson, J.P., et al. (2004). Forecasting Sclerotinia disease on lettuce: toward developing a prediction model for carpogenic germination of sclerotia. Phytopathology 94: 268–279.

Clarkson, S.J., Fawcett, L., Anthony, S.G. & Young, C. (2014). A model for Sclerotinia sclerotiorum infection and disease development in lettuce, based on temperature, relative humidity and ascospore density. PLoS ONE 9: e94049.

allii.tipo: Duthie's integrated equation (modified Weibull) with the original coefficients from Furuya et al. 2009.
allii.desc: Rust of garlic, onion and bunching onion. Furuya et al. (2009) modeled relative urediniospore infection on spring onion (Allium fistulosum) with Duthie's equation (modified Weibull form, R²=0.9369). A COOL-climate pathogen: optimum 10–15 °C and 100% RH; at 25 °C few uredinia form despite prolonged wetness. Pressure is therefore higher in autumn-winter and toward the southern end of the gradient.
vars: Favorability factor, cool optimum 10–15 °C; collapses above 20 °C.
Leaf-wetness proxy (RH≥90%). ≥4–6 h required; infection rises fast between 6.5 and 15 h.
logica: Integrated equation f(w,t) = f(t)·{1−exp[−(0.0877·w)^2.8087]} (wetness factor, eq. 4).
Duthie logistic f(t) with F=21.0547, G=1.0814 and time-varying H: H(w)=1.0196·e^(0.1231·w) (eq. 5, 6, 8).
Minimum wetness 5.25 h for any infection (none at 4 h).
Thermal optimum ~17.8 °C; collapses above 20 °C, near nil at 25 °C.
allii.supuestos: Wetness via RH≥90% proxy. Implements the ORIGINAL coefficients from Furuya et al. (2009) (Tables 2-3, eq. 4-6 and 8, time-varying H version, R²=0.9501) — not an approximation. Rust also depends on wind-borne long-distance inoculum (the model estimates the favorable window, not inoculum arrival). Original fit on bunching onion (A. fistulosum); on onion (A. cepa, more resistant) the extrapolation is conservative. Local calibration to Chile still pending.
refs: Furuya, H., Takanashi, H., Fuji, S., Nagai, Y. & Naito, H. (2009). Modeling Infection of Spring Onion by Puccinia allii in Response to Temperature and Leaf Wetness. Phytopathology 99(8): 951–956.
Duthie, J.A. (1997). Models of the response of foliar parasites to the combined effects of temperature and duration of wetness. Phytopathology 87: 1088–1095.
Pacific Northwest Pest Management Handbooks — Garlic/Onion Rust (Puccinia allii).

stemph.tipo: STEMcast 2.0: daily severity-value (DSV) matrix of T × wetness, onion-specific (Scicluna 2025).
stemph.desc: Stemphylium leaf blight of onion (SLB). STEMcast 2.0 is the onion-specific model developed in Ontario (derived from TOM-CAST, tuned to S. vesicarium). A WARM-wet pathogen: DSV only accumulate between 18 and 25 °C, with long wetness. Often accompanies purple blotch (Alternaria porri) as the 'purple blotch complex'.
vars: Leaf-wetness proxy (RH≥90%). Requires long wetness: ≥15 h at 18–20 °C, ≥13 h at 21–25 °C.
logica: STEMcast 2.0 matrix (Table 2.3, Scicluna 2025):
<18 °C or >25 °C: no accumulation (STEMcast 2.0 removes DSV below 18 °C).
Daily DSVs accumulate (CDSV); the trial action threshold was 15.
stemph.supuestos: Implements the REAL STEMcast 2.0 matrix (Scicluna 2025, Table 2.3), the most onion-specific Stemphylium model. KEY CAVEAT: in field trials (2023-2024) STEMcast 2.0 reduced fungicide applications but did NOT reduce SLB severity; the optimum T×wetness combinations for onion infection are not yet precisely known. Hence this output is a CONDUCIVE-WINDOW ALERT, not a severity predictor or a prescription. S. vesicarium is a weak pathogen that mostly infects through prior wounds (thrips, mildew/Alternaria lesions): a factor not modelled. Local calibration to Chile still pending.
refs: Scicluna, J. (2025). Determining Effective Management Strategies for Stemphylium Leaf Blight of Onion. MSc Thesis, University of Guelph, Ontario, Canada.
Kooy, M., et al. (2023b). STEMcast forecasting model for Stemphylium leaf blight of onion (desarrollo del modelo específico para cebolla, Holland Marsh, Ontario).
Pitblado, R.E. (1992). The development and implementation of TOM-CAST. Ontario Ministry of Agriculture and Food. (Base de la que deriva STEMcast.)
Montesinos, E., et al. (1995). Development and evaluation of an infection model for Stemphylium vesicarium on pear based on temperature and wetness duration. Phytopathology 85: 586–592.
